observations.summary: ChatList always pushes ChatDetail after a chat selection and only pops when the path stack size is greater than one. In split mode, Index switches Navigation to NavigationMode.Split for non-small breakpoints, so the active detail pane can have exactly one ChatDetail destination; selecting another chat pushes a second detail instead of replacing it. The repair owner is ChatList, where chat selection should replace/pop the active detail route on wide split layouts while preserving push behavior for small stack layouts.

In the split Navigation configured by Index.ets for non-small breakpoints, ChatList.ets handles every chat click by mutating currentChatId and calling pageInfo.pushPath({ name: 'ChatDetail' }). It only pops when pageInfo.size() > 1, so a wide-screen detail stack with a single active ChatDetail is not replaced; selecting another chat appends another ChatDetail destination. The repair owner is ChatList.ets, with behavior needing a wide-screen replace/pop-then-push path while preserving push stack navigation on small screens.

ChatList always calls pageInfo.pushPath({ name: 'ChatDetail' }) after changing currentChatId. Index switches Navigation to Split on non-small breakpoints, so repeated chat selection on wide screens appends another detail destination instead of replacing the existing detail pane. The repair belongs in ChatList selection routing: keep push behavior for small Stack mode, but replace or pop-and-push the active detail destination when currentBreakpoint is not small.

Chat selection in ChatList always pushes a new ChatDetail route after updating currentChatId. Index switches Navigation to Split mode for non-small breakpoints, so on wide screens that push adds another detail destination instead of replacing the active detail pane. Small-screen stack behavior should be preserved by making the replacement behavior conditional on the non-small split layout.

ChatList selection unconditionally calls pageInfo.pushPath({ name: 'ChatDetail' }) after only popping when stack size is greater than one. Index uses the same NavPathStack with NavigationMode.Split on non-small breakpoints, so wide-screen selection appends another detail route instead of replacing the active detail destination. The repair owner is ChatList: keep pushPath for small-screen stack navigation, but replace or reset the detail route for split breakpoints.

Chat selection in ChatList always mutates currentChatId and pushes a new ChatDetail route. Index switches Navigation to Split mode for non-small breakpoints, but ChatList does not branch on breakpoint or split mode to replace the active detail destination. The existing pushPath behavior is therefore correct for small-screen stack navigation but causes repeated detail destinations on wide split layouts.

The phone, verification-code, and password login buttons each run async authentication and route mutations directly from onClick without a shared pending guard or disabled/loading state, so repeated taps can start duplicate login/verifyCode/password calls and push or clear navigation multiple times.

The three login step components each run an async authentication call directly from Button.onClick without an in-flight guard or disabled pending state. Repeated taps can therefore issue duplicate login/verifyCode/password requests and repeat pageInfo.clear, pageInfo.pushPath, signOut, or login event emission, creating conflicting navigation transitions.

The phone, verification-code, and password login buttons each run async authentication and navigation logic directly from onClick without a shared pending guard or disabled state, allowing repeated taps to launch duplicate in-flight requests and duplicate pageInfo transitions.

Phone, verification-code, and password login buttons each run an async onClick that awaits login/verifyCode/password and then mutates NavPathStack or emits onLoginEvent, but none records or checks an in-flight pending state. Repeated taps can therefore start duplicate authentication requests and duplicate pushPath/clear/event navigation side effects. Index.ets confirms these login destinations are mounted through the shared Navigation stack and onLoginEvent triggers additional async login completion work.
Unable to inspect workspace before response.

The login action buttons in phone, verification-code, and password steps run async native authentication calls directly from onClick without a pending flag, early return, or disabled/loading binding. Repeated taps can therefore launch concurrent login/verifyCode/password requests and race their pageInfo.clear(), pushPath(), signOut(), and onLoginEvent navigation side effects.

WebM stickers are downloaded to local files, but MessageBubble reads the whole WebM into memory as Base64 and feeds the raw Base64 string to ArkWeb with MIME type audio/webm. The generated HTML helper is unused and does not provide a video source, so the embedded Web component never receives a playable video document. The repair owner is MessageBubble: load a real HTML video document or file-backed URL/stream with video/webm, avoiding full-file Base64 conversion.

WebM sticker rendering is handled in MessageBubble.ets by reading the whole local .webm into an ArrayBuffer, Base64-encoding it, and calling WebviewController.loadData with the raw Base64 payload as audio/webm/base64. The generated HTML/video path is not used, and the media is mislabeled as audio rather than rendered through a video element or local resource URL, explaining why an existing local downloaded WebM cannot render in the embedded Web component. This also violates the large-video Base64 constraint.

WebM sticker rendering is owned by MessageBubble. It detects local/downloaded .webm sticker files but reads the full video into an ArrayBuffer/Base64 string and calls ArkWeb loadData with raw base64 as audio/webm, while the generated HTML helper is unused and contains no video src. This violates the large-video Base64 constraint and leaves the embedded Web component without a proper HTML video resource/path to render.

WebM stickers are detected and downloaded to a local .webm path, but MessageBubble then reads the whole video into Base64 and calls WebviewController.loadData with the raw Base64 string as audio/webm. The generated HTML/video wrapper is not used, and the Web component is never given a renderable HTML document or local-file video source. This also violates the stated constraint by loading the full WebM into memory as Base64. The repair owner is MessageBubble.ets: use a Web-compatible local resource strategy or generated HTML referencing the local file/served URI without whole-file Base64 conversion, with the needed Web file/resource access settings.

WebM sticker rendering is owned by MessageBubble. The local .webm path is found/downloaded, but StickerView does not render the local file through a generated HTML video page; it converts the whole file to Base64 and calls WebviewController.loadData with the raw payload as audio/webm. The HTML generator is unused and contains no video src. Repair should stream/reference the local WebM through Web-compatible local resource handling instead of loading large videos as Base64.

WebM stickers are detected and downloaded to a local .webm path, but StickerView only renders the Web component when videoBase64 is populated. loadMedia reads the entire local WebM into an ArrayBuffer and Base64 string, then Web.onAppear calls loadData with the raw Base64 payload as audio/webm instead of loading an HTML page with a video element or a permitted local resource URL. This API misuse explains why an existing local WebM sticker cannot render from the embedded Web component, and it also violates the constraint against converting large videos to Base64 in memory.
